county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1854 from parts of Cumberland, Kennebec, Lincoln, and Oxford counties. Named for the Androscoggin River. Contains the twin cities of Lewiston-Auburn, major textile manufacturing center. Heavy French Canadian immigration to Lewiston mills from 1860s onward.
county_metadata.research_tips: Androscoggin County is the heart of Maine's Franco-American community. Lewiston's 'Little Canada' neighborhood housed thousands of Québécois mill workers — Catholic parish records at Sts. Peter and Paul (now Holy Cross parish) are essential, with registers in French. The Franco-American Heritage Center in Lewiston holds oral histories and genealogical files. For pre-1854 records, check Cumberland County (Auburn side) and Kennebec County (Lewiston side). The Androscoggin Historical Society in Auburn has local family files and business records from the textile era. Town clerks in Lewiston, Auburn, Lisbon, and Livermore Falls hold vital records from settlement. The Bates College archives contain materials on Lewiston social history.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Register of Deeds online portal

source_profiles.repository: Androscoggin County Register of Deeds, Auburn
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Pre-1854 deeds split among Cumberland, Kennebec, Lincoln, and Oxford counties.
Wills, administrations, guardianships. Pre-1854 probate in parent counties.
Town-level vital records for Lewiston, Auburn, Lisbon, etc. Published Maine vital records series.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — New England town clerk tradition.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1860-1950 (county formed 1854, first full census 1860).

source_profiles.source_name: Catholic Parish Records — Lewiston Franco-American Parishes
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Portland Archives / Franco-American Heritage Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, burial registers from Lewiston and Auburn Catholic parishes. French-language registers.
Major regional newspaper covering Lewiston-Auburn and surrounding towns. Obituaries, legal notices, community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good coverage on microfilm and digital.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention and petitions. Critical for French Canadian immigrant families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — some gaps in early records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions including Catholic cemeteries in Lewiston-Auburn area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good coverage through MOCA and Find A Grave.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Some early settlement records are sparse due to remote frontier conditions.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1839 from Penobscot and Washington counties. Largest county east of the Mississippi River. Named for the Aroostook River. Site of the Aroostook War (1838-39 border dispute with New Brunswick). Heavily Acadian and Québécois settlement in the St. John Valley. Potato farming economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Aroostook County research divides into two distinct cultural zones: the St. John Valley (Madawaska, Van Buren, Fort Kent, Frenchville) is overwhelmingly Acadian/French Canadian, while Houlton, Presque Isle, and Caribou reflect Anglo-American settlement. For the St. John Valley, Catholic parish records in French are primary sources — the Madawaska Historical Society and Acadian Archives at University of Maine Fort Kent are essential. Many families crossed freely between Maine and New Brunswick before borders hardened. The Aroostook County Registry of Deeds in Houlton has records from 1839 but the Northern Registry in Fort Kent handles St. John Valley deeds. NARA Waltham holds border-crossing records for Houlton, Van Buren, and Fort Fairfield ports of entry. The 1842 Webster-Ashburton Treaty fixed the border — earlier land claims are complex.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Register of Deeds portal; also Northern Registry in Fort Kent

source_profiles.repository: Aroostook County Register of Deeds, Houlton
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Northern Registry in Fort Kent handles St. John Valley records.
Town-level vital records. French-language records common in St. John Valley towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Variable — frontier conditions caused gaps in earliest records.

source_profiles.source_name: Catholic Parish Records — Aroostook Acadian Parishes
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Portland Archives / Acadian Archives UMFK
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Sacramental registers from Madawaska, Van Buren, Fort Kent, Frenchville parishes. French-language records essential for Acadian families.

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian Border Crossing Records — Aroostook Ports
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Border crossing records for Houlton, Van Buren, Fort Fairfield, and other Aroostook ports of entry. Critical for tracking cross-border French Canadian movement.
Federal population schedules 1840-1950. 1840 is first census after county formation. French-speaking enumerations in St. John Valley.

source_profiles.source_name: Aroostook War Records and Civil War Service
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Aroostook War militia records (1838-39) and Civil War service records. Many early settlers had militia service during border dispute.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial for Aroostook War; good for Civil War.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Land Office — Aroostook Settling Lands
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State land grants and settling lots in Aroostook County. Lottery lands and road-building grants that encouraged northern settlement.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — Maine State Archives holds originals.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant county-level record losses. Individual Portland town records suffered some losses in the Great Fire of 1866.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Maine's two original counties (with Lincoln), formed 1760 from York County while still part of Massachusetts. Contains Portland, Maine's largest city. Named for William, Duke of Cumberland. Portland was destroyed by British bombardment (1775) and the Great Fire of 1866.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cumberland County is Maine's most populous and record-rich county. Portland city records begin in the 1630s (as Falmouth/Casco). The Great Fire of July 4, 1866 destroyed much of Portland's commercial district but most vital records survived because town clerk records were in the city hall vault. The Maine Historical Society in Portland is the premier genealogical repository — their research library holds family papers, church records, and manuscript collections spanning three centuries. Portland's diverse immigration included Irish (from the 1840s), Italian, Jewish, and later Southeast Asian communities. The Cumberland County Registry of Deeds has records from 1760 (earlier in York County). Probate records are exceptionally complete. The Greater Portland Landmarks and Portland Public Library hold architectural and neighborhood history files useful for placing families in specific addresses.
county_metadata.heritage_book: William Willis, 'The History of Portland' (1831-33, revised 1865)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index and images via county portal

source_profiles.repository: Cumberland County Registry of Deeds, Portland
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1760. Pre-1760 in York County. Exceptionally complete grantor/grantee indexes.
Wills, administrations, guardianships, inventories from 1760. Pre-1760 probate in York County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — complete files with original documents.

source_profiles.source_name: Portland and Cumberland County Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records from earliest settlement. Portland (Falmouth) records from 1630s.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Very good — some gaps from colonial-era disruptions.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1790-1950 (pre-1820 as Massachusetts).
Major Portland newspapers. Eastern Argus (1803-1921) is key early source. Portland Press Herald (1921-present).
Records of Portland's oldest church and other Congregational, Episcopal, Catholic parishes. MHS holds extensive church record collections.
State death registrations from 1892. Earlier deaths in town clerk records.
Naturalization records from federal and state courts in Portland. Heavy Irish immigration from 1840s.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1838 from parts of Kennebec, Oxford, and Somerset counties. Named for Benjamin Franklin. Rural western Maine county. Farmington is the county seat and home of University of Maine at Farmington. Sandy River and Rangeley Lakes Railroad served the region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Franklin County is rural western Maine with records split among three parent counties before 1838. For pre-1838 research, determine which parent county held jurisdiction for your specific town: check Kennebec County for the eastern towns, Oxford County for the southwestern towns, and Somerset County for the northern portions. The Franklin County Registry of Deeds in Farmington has complete records from 1838. University of Maine at Farmington's Mantor Library holds local history collections including Sandy River railroad records and logging company documents. The Farmington Historical Society and Nordica Memorial Association (birthplace of opera singer Lillian Nordica) have local family files. Town histories for Farmington, Wilton, Jay, and Rangeley are key resources available at Maine State Library.

source_profiles.repository: Franklin County Register of Deeds, Farmington
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1838. Pre-1838 in Kennebec, Oxford, and Somerset counties.
Wills, administrations, guardianships from 1838.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Franklin County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Farmington, Wilton, Jay, Strong, and other Franklin County towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — New England town clerk tradition.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1840-1950. 1840 is first census after county formation.
Transcriptions from rural town cemeteries. Many small family burying grounds in farmland.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — MOCA volumes and Find A Grave.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property tax valuations useful for tracking land ownership before and after deed transfers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1789 from Lincoln County while Maine was part of Massachusetts. Named for John Hancock, Governor of Massachusetts. Contains Mount Desert Island (Acadia National Park), Bar Harbor, and Blue Hill. Major maritime and fishing economy historically.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hancock County is coastal Downeast Maine with deep maritime heritage. The Registry of Deeds in Ellsworth has records from 1789 but for pre-1789 research, Lincoln County (Wiscasset) is essential. Bar Harbor and Mount Desert Island records include the summer colony of wealthy families (Rockefeller, Ford, Vanderbilt) from the 1880s — estate records and land deeds for this period are unusually detailed. The Great Fire of 1947 on Mount Desert Island destroyed many Bar Harbor mansions but town records survived. The Ellsworth American newspaper (1855-present) is a key source for obituaries and legal notices. Hancock County Historical Society and the Blue Hill Historical Society hold local family papers. Maritime records — ship registrations, crew lists, and fishing vessel documentation — are at NARA Waltham. Deer Isle granite quarry employment records document immigrant stonecutters from Scotland and Italy.

source_profiles.repository: Hancock County Register of Deeds, Ellsworth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1789. Pre-1789 in Lincoln County.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1789. Includes detailed estate inventories for wealthy summer residents.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Hancock County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Ellsworth, Blue Hill, Castine, Deer Isle, Mount Desert, and other towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — some early coastal town records fragmented.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1790-1950. Pre-1820 enumerated as Massachusetts.
Primary regional newspaper for Hancock County. Obituaries, probate notices, shipping news.
Congregational, Baptist, and Unitarian church records from coastal towns. Castine records date to colonial period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Variable — some churches deposited records at MHS.

source_profiles.source_name: Civil War Service Records — Hancock County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hancock County men served in multiple Maine regiments. Muster rolls and service records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1799 from Lincoln County. Contains Augusta, the state capital (since 1832). Named for the Kennebec River. Kennebec Purchase proprietary records are important colonial-era sources. Waterville is home to Colby College.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kennebec County contains the state capital Augusta and is central to Maine genealogical research because the Maine State Archives and Maine State Library are both located here. The Kennebec Purchase Company records (colonial-era land speculation covering much of central Maine) are at Maine State Archives and Massachusetts Historical Society — these proprietary records predate county deed records. The Registry of Deeds in Augusta has records from 1799; pre-1799 check Lincoln County in Wiscasset. Augusta's Lithgow Library and Waterville Public Library hold local genealogical collections. The Maine State Museum has exhibits and records on the Kennebec Valley. Colby College Special Collections in Waterville hold regional manuscript collections. Town histories for Augusta, Hallowell, Gardiner, and Waterville are essential — Hallowell was commercially important before Augusta became the capital. The Kennebec Historical Society actively collects family papers.

source_profiles.repository: Kennebec County Register of Deeds, Augusta
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1799. Pre-1799 in Lincoln County. Kennebec Purchase Company records predate county records.
Wills, administrations, guardianships from 1799. State capital location means well-maintained records.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Kennebec County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Augusta, Hallowell, Gardiner, Waterville, and other towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Very good — capital county records well preserved.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1800-1950. County formed 1799, first full census 1800.
Superior and district court records including civil and criminal cases, naturalization proceedings.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — deposited at Maine State Archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: State death registrations from 1892. Earlier deaths recorded by town clerks.
Long-running Augusta newspaper. State capital reporting, obituaries, legislative news, legal notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — microfilmed at Maine State Library.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1860 from Lincoln and Waldo counties. Named for Henry Knox, first U.S. Secretary of War, who owned vast tracts (the Knox Patent) in the Thomaston area. Rockland is the county seat. Major lime-burning and granite quarrying industry.
county_metadata.research_tips: Knox County was carved from Lincoln and Waldo counties in 1860, so earlier records split between Wiscasset (Lincoln) and Belfast (Waldo). The Knox Patent (Waldo Patent) — Henry Knox's vast landholding — generated unique land records held at Maine State Archives and the Knox Museum (Montpelier) in Thomaston. Rockland's lime industry attracted immigrant workers from Nova Scotia, Sweden, and Finland; employment records and union documents supplement genealogical research. The Penobscot Marine Museum in Searsport (just across the Waldo line) and Rockland's Shore Village Museum have maritime records. The Knox County Registry of Deeds in Rockland has records from 1860. Vinalhaven granite quarry records document Scottish and Italian stonecutters. The Camden-Rockport Historical Society and Thomaston Historical Society hold local family files and photographs.

source_profiles.repository: Knox County Register of Deeds, Rockland
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1860. Pre-1860 split between Lincoln (Wiscasset) and Waldo (Belfast) counties.
Wills, estates from 1860. Earlier probate in Lincoln and Waldo counties.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Knox County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Rockland, Camden, Thomaston, Vinalhaven, and other Knox County towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — New England town clerk tradition.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules from 1860 (county formation year). Earlier censuses under Lincoln or Waldo.
Cemetery transcriptions including maritime family burial grounds. Thomaston and Rockland cemeteries well covered.
Primary Knox County newspaper. Shipping news, lime kiln reports, obituaries, legal notices.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Lincoln County records are exceptionally important as parent county records for many other Maine counties.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Maine's two original counties (with Cumberland), formed 1760 from York County. Originally covered most of eastern and central Maine. Parent county for Hancock, Washington, Kennebec, Oxford, Somerset, Penobscot, and indirectly many others. Wiscasset is the county seat.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County is the most critical parent county in Maine genealogy. When formed in 1760, it encompassed virtually all of Maine east of the Cumberland County line. Hancock, Washington, Kennebec, Oxford, Somerset, and Penobscot counties were all carved from Lincoln — and daughter counties were carved from those. Any research in central or eastern Maine before 1789-1816 must check Lincoln County records in Wiscasset. The Lincoln County Registry of Deeds has records from 1760 and earlier York County transfers. The Lincoln County Probate Court records are among the oldest in Maine. The Pownalborough Court House (1761) in Dresden is the oldest surviving pre-Revolutionary courthouse in Maine and has been preserved as a museum. Wiscasset Public Library and the Lincoln County Historical Association hold extensive local history collections. Newcastle, Damariscotta, and Boothbay town records are rich sources for early coastal settlement.

source_profiles.repository: Lincoln County Registry of Deeds, Wiscasset
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1760. Critical parent county — earlier deeds in York County. Records for what later became many other Maine counties.
Among the oldest probate records in Maine. Pre-1760 probate in York County. Parent jurisdiction for much of Maine.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Lincoln County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level records for Wiscasset, Newcastle, Damariscotta, Boothbay, and other towns. Some records date to earliest settlement.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Very good — among Maine's oldest vital records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1790-1950. Note: Lincoln County territory shrank dramatically over time as daughter counties formed.
Court of Common Pleas and Supreme Judicial Court records. Pownalborough Court House (1761) records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — partially at Maine State Archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Congregational and other church records from early coastal towns. Walpole Meeting House, Round Pond Baptist Church, etc.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Variable — some at MHS, some with local congregations.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Some of Maine's oldest burying grounds. Colonial-era stones in Newcastle, Damariscotta, and Pemaquid area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — MOCA transcriptions and Find A Grave.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1805 from York and Cumberland counties. Western Maine county bordering New Hampshire. Named for Oxford, Massachusetts, hometown of many settlers. South Paris (in the town of Paris) is the county seat. Fryeburg is the oldest town.
county_metadata.research_tips: Oxford County spans western Maine along the New Hampshire border and is rich in early settlement records. For pre-1805 research, check York County (for the southwestern towns like Fryeburg, Brownfield, and Hiram) and Cumberland County (for the eastern towns). The Fryeburg area was settled in the 1760s and has exceptionally early records — Fryeburg Academy archives hold local history materials. The Oxford County Registry of Deeds in South Paris has records from 1805. The Bethel Historical Society has outstanding collections for the Androscoggin River valley portion of the county. Norway and South Paris were manufacturing centers with shoe factory records. Rumford Falls paper mill records (1890s-onward) document immigrant workers from Finland, Italy, and French Canada. The Maine-New Hampshire border means some families appear in records on both sides — check Carroll and Coos County NH records for border-area families.

source_profiles.repository: Oxford County Register of Deeds, South Paris
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1805. Pre-1805 in York and Cumberland counties.

source_profiles.repository: Oxford County Probate Court, South Paris
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations from 1805. Earlier probate in York and Cumberland counties.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Oxford County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Paris, Norway, Fryeburg, Bethel, Rumford, and other towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — Fryeburg records among Maine's earliest.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1810-1950. County formed 1805, first full census 1810.
Congregational, Baptist, and Universalist church records from western Maine towns.
Primary Oxford County newspaper. Obituaries, legal notices, town meeting reports.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1816 from Hancock County while still part of Massachusetts. Contains Bangor, formerly 'Lumber Capital of the World.' Named for the Penobscot River and Penobscot Nation. University of Maine main campus in Orono. Indian Island (Penobscot Nation reservation) in Old Town.
county_metadata.research_tips: Penobscot County centers on Bangor, which was the world's largest lumber port in the mid-1800s. Lumbering records — boom company logs, river drive payrolls, and timber land surveys — are at the University of Maine Fogler Library and the Bangor Public Library's Special Collections. For pre-1816 records check Hancock County in Ellsworth. The Penobscot Nation on Indian Island has tribal records — the Penobscot Nation Museum and tribal enrollment office hold genealogical materials distinct from county records; BIA records at NARA Waltham are also relevant. Bangor Theological Seminary records (1814-2013) include pastoral records for Congregational churches across northern Maine. Bangor attracted Irish immigrants (canal and railroad workers) and later French Canadians. The Bangor Historical Society has extensive family files and photograph collections. Mount Hope Cemetery in Bangor has records from 1834.

source_profiles.repository: Penobscot County Register of Deeds, Bangor
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1816. Pre-1816 in Hancock County. Extensive timber land transactions.
Wills, administrations from 1816. Some of Maine's wealthiest lumber baron estates.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Penobscot County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Bangor, Orono, Old Town, Brewer, and other Penobscot County towns.
Federal population schedules 1820-1950. 1820 is first census after both county formation and Maine statehood.

source_profiles.repository: University of Maine / Maine State Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Major regional newspaper for northern Maine. Bangor Whig and Courier (1834-1900), Bangor Daily News (1889-present).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — extensive microfilm and digital archives at UMaine.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions including Mount Hope Cemetery (1834), one of the oldest garden cemeteries in the US.
State marriage registrations from 1892. Earlier marriages recorded by town clerks and ministers.
